---
title: "Lonely in London"
subtitle: "An AI-written qualitative paper on loneliness among young adults </br>Steve Powell, steve@causalmap.app"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    transition: slide
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: false
    smaller: true
    css: slides-mermaid.css
    mermaid:
      theme: dark
---

## My motivation {.center}

::: {.r-fit-text}
A bit of scepticism
:::

- Background 
  - Reproducible/verifiable qual and quant research for project evaluation
  - Qualitative causal mapping
  - AI for qualitative research
- Impressed by Susanne's case for conversational approach as a way to build theory and get reflexive depth
- But, do you need a human? 

---

# The experiment {.center}

::: {.r-fit-text}
Human wrapper + AI inner paper
:::

Topic: Loneliness among young adults in London

- 48 interviews, 4 deprived London boroughs, reflexive thematic analysis
- I have no "skin in the game"

- Final paper: https://garden.causalmap.app/lonely

- Public GitHub repository: https://github.com/stevepowell99/agents4qual2


![](static/img/image-20260313081631653.png)



---

![](static/img/image-20260312220455877.png)
---

---

### The Cursor workspace (a coding app, not QDA software)

![](static/img/image-20260313080851739.png)

Cursor provides a single agent within the workspace, which can access the source files and create and update memo files. In this case its model was GPT-5.1.

---


# Substantive findings (AI paper)

## Themes and mechanisms

| Theme | Mechanism | Example |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| **Misrecognition** | Near others yet unseen | "I've always had people around me physically, but I haven't felt like they can understand" |
| **Place** | Crowded anonymity, threat | "Better lonely than scared" |
| **Infrastructure** | Low-judgement repeated contact | "I sometimes prefer opening up to strangers because they don't know me as much" |
| **Filters** | Money, apps | "They know that I don't have money" |

**Implication**: Build low-cost, low-judgement spaces; address safety and mobility as gating conditions.

---

## Connection infrastructure

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Thick**
- Teams, centres, faith communities
- Ongoing ties, advice networks

**Thin**
- Everyday third spaces
- Known strangers (gym, hairdresser)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 320
flowchart LR
    A[Structure] --> B[Shared purpose]
    A --> C[Predictable norms]
    A --> D[Repeated contact]
    B --> E[Lower judgement cost]
    C --> E
    D --> E
```
:::
:::

Relief ≠ deep intimacy. Minimal unit: recognisability, low-stakes talk.

---

# Evaluation


---

## Reflection

::: {.r-stack}
::: {.fragment .current-visible}
> It worked 🎇🎇! It really developed a theory iteratively!
:::
::: {.fragment .current-visible}
> I enjoyed watching Cursor work, step by step creating and updating its memos.
:::
::: {.fragment .current-visible}
> I find the paper unreadable.
:::
::: {.fragment .current-visible}
> Lack of skin in the game: I don't care.
:::
::: {.fragment}
> Easy to think 'it looks systematic, so it must be right'.
:::
:::


---

## WEIRD 

Like all such methods, extremely exposed to the WEIRDness of the training data.

---

## (Alternative approach: causal mapping)

![](static/img/image-20260312221338890.png)

Top 30 links. Completely automated/generic approach. 30 minutes to auto-code. 5 minutes to filter. Much less exposure to LLM world-view.

Bookmark [#1319](https://app.causalmap.app/?bookmark=1319) 


---

## Finally

- Yes an AI can iterate a theory using memos
- You don't need a human to generate a theory
- The process was lacking "skin in the game"
- Could we give the AI some skin in the game? Not at the moment.
- Could we give the AI the opportunity to reflect? Absolutely.
- This automated approach is a bit more reproducible and auditable
- Still very exposed to WEIRDness

steve@causalmap.app

app.causalmap.app

**GitHub**: [github.com/stevepowell99/agents4qual2](https://github.com/stevepowell99/agents4qual2)





--------

# Appendix 

--------



## Four mechanism themes {#four-mechanism-themes}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 500
%%| fig-height: 4.5
flowchart TB
    subgraph Themes
        T1[Misrecognition]
        T2[Place constraint]
        T3[Connection infrastructure]
        T4[Digital and material filters]
    end
    
    T1 --> T3
    T2 --> T3
    T4 -.-> T1
    T4 -.-> T3
```

Boundary conditions matter: same place/platform → opposite outcomes across accounts.


---

## Response to reviewers (1)

**Errors in AI's inner paper — already reported**

- Journal not maintained as claimed; two quote misattributions
- I will *not* correct the inner paper: the experiment presents AI output as written; reflection documents its errors

**Errors I did not notice — will add to wrapper**

- n=16 per borough vs 48 total (arithmetic: 16×4=64)

**Errors/omissions in my wrapper**

- Anonymity, checklist, formatting, authorship note, model transparency, ethics of AI processing, autoethnographic depth

---

## Response to reviewers (2)

**Where both I and reviewers were wrong**

- Inner paper says "**deliberate** negative-case search" (3.3, 3.4) — never "systematic"
- My reflection incorrectly stated it claimed systematic; reviewers echoed that
- I will correct my reflection

**Incorrect reviewer criticisms**

- Distinguishing AI inner paper vs human wrapper avoids absurdity of "correcting" what I already disclosed

---

![](static/img/image-20260312213237815.png)


---

## Takeaways

1. **Cursor's affordance**: Self-updating memo files enable iterative thematic analysis beyond single-prompt approaches
2. **Substantive contribution**: Loneliness as infrastructure + constraint, not individual deficit
3. **Transparency**: Quote-checking and methodology audit revealed overclaiming — valuable for AI-in-research reporting
4. **Design target**: "Safer and more predictable interaction with lower judgement costs"


**Filename:** lonely-in-london. **Coverage:** Citations - 1099 / 3392 (32%). Sources - 50 / 50 (100%). **Factors** — size: citation count; numbers: source count; colour: outcomeness; border: avg incoming sentiment (blue=positive, grey=neutral, red=negative). **Links** — width: citation count; labels: source count; arrowheads: effect sentiment (blue=positive, grey=neutral, red=negative). **Filters applied:** Soft recode: 28 magnets, similarity >= 0.48; Link freq: top 30 by citation count.
